label: Social media
title: Growing an account: the non-negotiable basics
desc: Consistency, volume of tests, analyzing winners — what you need to set up before looking for the "right video".
contentHtml:
Growth doesn't come from one perfect video. It comes from a repeated system that lets the algorithm understand your account and test it on different audiences.

Post every day

An account that posts rarely stays invisible. Consistency lets the algorithm test the account on multiple audiences. The goal isn't to make a perfect video — it's to create enough volume to find what sticks.

Test lots of formats

One video proves nothing. You need to test multiple hooks, angles, lengths, CTAs, setups, and topics. What works shows up through repetition: views, retention, comments, shares, bio clicks.

Analyze the winners

When a video performs, you need to understand why. Strong hook? Very clear problem? Line that triggers a reaction? Controversial topic? Satisfying visual demo? Simple promise? Then, spin the same mechanism — don't just remake the same video.

Have a clear direction

An account that talks about everything sells nothing. The audience needs to quickly understand:

Who you helpWhat problem you solveWhy follow youWhere to click

Simple rule

Post every day → test a lot → keep what works → improve hooks → create reactions → stay consistent on your target.

label: Social media
title: Hook: stop the scroll in 2 seconds
desc: The 6 types of hooks that work, what makes them effective, and how to adapt them to your niche.
contentHtml:
The first 1 to 2 seconds decide whether someone stays or swipes. No slow intro, no "hey guys", no unnecessary context. A good hook opens tension the viewer wants to see resolved.

The 6 types of hooks that work

Direct problem

"If you have this problem…"

Common mistake

"The mistake everyone makes…"

Broken belief

"No, it's not X, it's Y."

Visible result

"Before / after…"

Curiosity

"Watch what happens when…"

Concrete gain

"Here's how to avoid losing…"

Real-world examples

"If your heating does this, it's not normal."
"Most tradespeople fail their videos for this reason."
"You think your problem comes from X, but it's actually Y."
"Here's why nobody clicks on your profile."

What makes a hook ineffective

If someone has to think to understand, they swipe. The hook should sound like a real line from the field, not like abstract marketing copy.

Key takeaway

A good hook doesn't explain — it creates tension. The answer comes in the next 30 seconds. If your hook is too complete, you have nothing left to show.

label: Social media
title: The 5 video structures that work
desc: Problem → solution, false belief, demonstration, transformation, short storytelling — with concrete examples for each.
contentHtml:
A video without structure runs out of steam halfway through. These 5 structures are proven — no need to invent new ones, just master them.

1. Problem → Explanation → Solution → CTA

The most robust foundation for short videos.

"If your lock is stuck…" [problem]
"It's not necessarily the lock that's dead…" [explanation]
"Often, all it takes is…" [solution]
"If you want me to look at your case, link in bio." [CTA]

2. False belief → Truth → Proof

Very effective for driving reactions. The viewer feels concerned or corrected.

"No, a cloudy pool doesn't necessarily mean you have to drain everything."
"The real problem often comes from water balance."
"Look here…"

3. Demonstration

You show a gesture, a tool, a mistake, or a result. Works very well for tradespeople, technical jobs, physical products. The visual does part of the work.

4. Transformation

Before → Action → Result. The brain loves seeing progress.

Garden transformingRoof replacedEngine rebuiltRoom renovated

5. Short storytelling

Initial situation → Tension → Resolution.

"A client calls me because they think they need to change everything…"
"Actually, the problem was just…"
"Result: simple repair instead of a big bill."

Key takeaway

One idea per video. One clear problem. One visual proof. One logical CTA. No unnecessary filler.

label: Social media
title: Create content that drives reactions and helps the algo
desc: Retention, comments, shares, saves — the 4 signals that push a video and how to trigger them.
contentHtml:
The algorithm doesn't push videos at random. It measures specific signals. Here's how to trigger them intentionally.

The 4 signals that matter

⏱

Retention

Watch to the end

💬

Comments

Reaction = reach

🔁

Shares

Very strong signal

🔖

Saves

Useful content

What generates comments

MistakesPricesStrong opinionsRelatable situations"What would you do?"Impressive before/after

Stand behind a line that slightly divides: "Stop doing this, you're damaging your engine." or "The cheapest option often costs more in the end."

What generates shares

Practical tipsMistakes to avoidChecklistsAlertsUseful to send to someone

What generates saves

"3 things to check before calling a repair person."
"Signs your engine has a problem."
"Checklist before redoing your pool."

What maximizes retention

Fast hookClear tensionShort videoNo dead timeAnswer at the end

Key takeaway

A concrete case always drives more reactions than general advice. The more the scene looks like the audience's real life, the more they react.

label: Social media
title: Position your account and your sales funnel
desc: Bio that sells, bio link that converts, CTA that matches the offer — the complete logic from account to action.
contentHtml:
A video that gets views but doesn't sell is often a funnel problem — not a content problem. Here's how to align every link in the chain.

The account as a mini sales funnel

🎬 Video

Grabs attention

👤 Profile

Reassures and clarifies

📝 Bio

Explains the offer

🔗 Bio link

Turns interest into action

A bio that sells — not a bio that decorates

A good bio answers 3 questions in 2 lines:

Who you helpWhat result you deliverHow to take action

❌ Avoid

"Passionate about innovation."
"Content creator."
"Ambitious entrepreneur."

✓ What works

"I help tradespeople attract more clients with short videos."
"AI video creation — quote via the link."

The bio link — one main CTA

The visitor must understand in under 5 seconds where to click. Too many options kills action. The link must match the video CTA exactly:

If the video says "link in bio for a quote" → first button = "Request a quote"
If the video says "see the examples" → first button leads to examples
If the video says "book your call" → direct link to scheduling

Precise CTA — not vague

❌ Too vague

"Subscribe."
"Click in bio."
"Contact me."

✓ Precise

"If you want a quote, click the first link."
"Send me this word in DM."

Key takeaway

The CTA must be consistent with the video. Advice video → CTA to diagnostic. Product video → CTA to purchase. Demo video → CTA to quote.

label: Social media
title: Research and inspiration: learn the codes before innovating
desc: How to analyze top-performing accounts, extract the mechanics that work, and adapt them to your niche without copying.
contentHtml:
Trying to be original too early is often a mistake. You first need to understand the codes that already work — only then twist them your own way.

What to analyze on top-performing accounts

The hooks

"If you do this…"
"Nobody tells you this…"
"Before paying for X…"

The angles

Mistake to avoid / Quick tip / Demo / Before-after / Client case

The CTAs

"DM me the word X"
"Comment X"
"Book your call"

The formats

Length, editing style, offer type, comments generated

Adapt, don't copy

Original hook: "If your site doesn't convert, it's not a design problem."
Tradesperson adaptation: "If your pool stays cloudy, it's not necessarily a filtration problem."

The Gemini trick for analyzing a video

Record a video you like on your phone. Send it to Gemini and ask it to analyze how the creator got the idea and how to adapt the mechanic to your type of content. Having a clear message remains the foundation.

Build an inspiration library

Save interesting videos and sort them by:

HookAngleFormatCTALengthEditing styleOffer type

The rule

Innovating without understanding the codes = luck. Innovating after understanding what makes people stop, watch, comment, click, and buy = strategy.

label: Field Guide
title: My journey: from 300 views to 1 million
desc: What really worked on ViralWorks Studio — the formats tested, the mistakes, the lucky break, and how I turned it into a system.
contentHtml:
Field experience

This guide is different from the others. It's direct feedback from creating ViralWorks Studio content — not theory, but the real decisions that led to the results.

The starting point

At the start: a few hundred views per video, no concrete results. The central question was: how to reach as many people as possible with content that's still oriented toward the product I want to highlight?

The "AI Expert Tip" format

The idea: give advice to everyday people by featuring a professional generated with AI. An expert who seems to actually exist. At first, two problems at once:

Finding the right camera angleSetups too complex to execute

I wanted to make a magnet that pulls all the screws from a caliper at once with a funny line from the expert. Technically impossible with the tools available at the time.

The decision that changed everything

Radically simplify: the entire video based only on the tip, with no complex setup. Just make the AI expert pass for a real expert. It worked — but not right away.

~20k

Plateau of first videos

300k

Object substitution video

600k

Same character, simple video

1M+

3 similar videos after that

The lucky break — and what it taught

The video with a watering can instead of WD40 got 300,000 views. A lucky break — but the character clicked. By redoing a much simpler video with the same character: 600,000 views. The character had built familiarity. Then: 3 similar videos at 1 million each.

What it proves

Sometimes you're not far from making it despite all the good advice. It's a matter of perseverance and slight tweaks — without completely changing format every 2 days.

The new format adopted

Videos with the AI avatar in 30-sec mini film scenes. It doesn't hit a million — but the audience from the "Expert" videos goes to the profile and watches the other videos, which boosts views on content that gets fewer on its own. One format feeds the other.

The lesson

It's not a perfect format that creates growth. It's a format you keep refining until you find what resonates — and then you exploit it systematically.

label: AI Creation
title: The 5 video format families
desc: Product, Storytelling, Human, Process, Social native — all the categories so you never run out of ideas.
contentHtml:
Creative block doesn't really exist — it comes from not having a list of formats in front of you. Here are the 5 families that cover the essentials.

📦 Product

Aesthetic adDemonstrationUnboxingTest / ReviewComparisonDetail focusPerformance proofProduct reveal

🎭 Storytelling

TV SpotProblem → SolutionScenario setupClient storyOrigin storyFuture scenario

👤 Human

Expert face camTestimonialInterviewFAQMyth vs realityReaction video

🔧 Process / Transformation

Trade gestureTimelapseBefore / AfterBehind the scenesStep-by-stepMistake → Fix

📱 Social native

Vlog POVShort educational hookChallengeTrend twistComment replyQuick list

The real variable

It's not the format that changes — it's the angle, the hook, and the combination between two formats. Don't look for a new format. Master the ones that exist.

label: AI Creation
title: Understanding AI video engines
desc: Veo3, Kling, Hailuo, Higgsfield — what each tool actually does, and why it changes everything about how you prompt.
contentHtml:
An AI video lasts between 3 and 15 seconds depending on the platform. Veo3 offers 4/6/8 sec. Generation takes between 30 sec and 15 min. The vaguer your prompt, the more the AI fills in with its imagination.

The First Frame / Last Frame principle

By providing a start image and an end image, you frame what the AI can imagine and avoid surprises.

Diagram — First Frame → video → Last Frame

Veo3 vs Kling

Veo3

Strong on natural look and overall consistency. Limited on timing precision. For 3 different shots → 3 separate videos to assemble. Cost: €0.30 to €1/video.

Kling

Accepts second-by-second instructions. Supports the @image system. Better for precise multi-shot setups.

Tools to know

Veo3 via Flow LabsVeo3 via Vertex AIKlingHiggsfieldHailuo

label: AI Creation
title: Create an effective image prompt
desc: Hailuo/Nanobananapro structure and Kling structure — the two templates to know for precise reference images.
contentHtml:
The prompt remains the key. Two different structures depending on the platform.

For Hailuo and Nanobananapro

Turn my idea into an ultra-realistic prompt in English for Hailuo.
Here's my idea: [describe the idea].
The result should describe:
– Format & style (vertical vlog 9:16, ultra-realistic, cinematic)
– The camera (POV, selfie, lens type, stability)
– The subject (appearance, clothing, visual details)
– The environment (location, light, visual mood)
– Mood & emotions (atmosphere, sounds)
– Extras (natural movements, textures)

For Kling — advanced structure

Technical Configuration (mode, duration, ratio, character reference) + Prompt block (Scene / Subject / Timeline / Camera / Light / Audio) + Power-Up Specs + universal Negative Prompt.

The Kling @image trick

Kling lets you attach multiple reference images and call them with @image1, @image2… directly in the prompt to specify which visual plays which role.

Nanobananapro watermark

Use the Magic Eraser in Canva Pro — select the watermark, Canva rebuilds the background automatically.

label: Avatar & Product
title: Create your consistent AI avatar
desc: How to build a reusable character across all your videos — with or without yourself as the base.
contentHtml:
The goal: a character whose outfit, setup, and mood you can change — without ever losing visual consistency between videos.

Step 1 — Take the base photos

Take photos of yourself from multiple angles with your iPhone: left profile, front, right profile, waist shot, full body. No studio needed.

Step 2 — Place the character on a white background with Kling

For each photo, send it to Kling asking to keep facial and body features, white background, desired outfit. Result: clean, reusable "character sheets".

Step 3 — Create the triptych reference image

Assemble 3 cleaned photos in a 3-column image via Kling with @image1, @image2, @image3. This composite image becomes your main reference.

Example — triptych image with 3 angles of the same character

Key takeaway

Long to do the first time. But once the sheets are created, changing outfit or mood takes a few minutes.

label: Avatar & Product
title: Product photo shoot with AI
desc: Create professional product visuals without a studio — starting from just a photo of the product.
contentHtml:
One reference photo is enough to generate dozens of different setups. The same logic as the avatar applies to a product.

The process

You give Kling an image of your product as @image1, you describe the setup: background, mood, lighting, camera angle. The AI keeps the product and places it in the requested setting.

Example — Dior perfume, minimalist studio mood

Vary the moods

On the same product reference, chain very different moods by changing just @image2 as a background reference: clean studio, wild nature, urban neon, dramatic low angle.

Key takeaway

The possibilities are only limited by imagination — and prompt quality. A precise prompt on camera angle completely changes the result.

label: AI Creation
title: From image to video — Veo3 and Kling
desc: image-to-video, subject-to-video, complete Veo3 prompt — how to bring your images to life.
contentHtml:
Once images are ready, two main modes: image-to-video (you provide scene start and/or end) and subject-to-video (you provide a character or product, the AI builds the scene around it).

Veo3 prompt

Write me a prompt for VEO3 in English except dialogue (in French),
keeping the style of the image sent and this structure:

Ultra realistic cinematic chaotic vlog shot
Idea: [your idea]
Style: / Camera: / Lighting: / Environment: / Tone:
Dialogue in French: [if needed]
Important: clear action, mood, no exclamation points,
the video must last 8 seconds

The Veo3 limit on multi-shots

For a dynamic video with 3 shots: generate 3 separate 4 sec videos and assemble in editing. Cost: €0.30 to €1 per generation.

Kling for precise setups

Kling accepts second-by-second instructions. To go further: Cursor or Claude Code with "skills" — scripts that automate video prompt construction.

Tools to know

Veo3 via Flow LabsVeo3 via Vertex AIKlingHiggsfield

label: Editing & Voice
title: Edit and finalize with CapCut
desc: Importing scenes, transitions, sound effects, audio sync — the complete editing workflow for a publishable video.
contentHtml:
CapCut Pro is more than enough to assemble clean, effective AI videos. Cut rhythm and music matter as much as image quality.

Multi-scene importTransitionsSound effectsMusic importVoice importAudio/video syncBasic motion design
Screenshot — CapCut timeline with assembled AI scenes

Recommended workflow

1. Import all scenes in order. 2. Cut / adjust each shot's duration. 3. Add transitions. 4. Import music and adjust level. 5. Add voice. 6. Sound effects if needed. 7. Export in 1080p.

Key takeaway

Editing is what turns decent scenes into a video people want to watch to the end.

label: Editing & Voice
title: Clone a voice with ElevenLabs
desc: Instant Voice Clone and Voice Changer — keep the same voice across all your scenes without re-recording every time.
contentHtml:
For your avatar to stay consistent from one video to the next, the voice must be as constant as the face.

Option 1 — Instant Voice Clone

You import at least 10 seconds of your voice, ElevenLabs creates a voice model. You then use it to generate text read in that voice.

Screenshot — ElevenLabs Instant Voice Clone interface

Option 2 — Voice Changer

You import an AI video with a voice you like. Voice Changer replaces the video's voice with the model's. Result: same voice, new scene.

Key settings

Stability: too high = robotic voice, too low = uneven. Similarity: keep high for consistency across scenes.
